item.title: 19km road into Telefomin opened
item.description: FOR the first time, vehicles can now reach Tifalmin in Telefomin, West Sepik, following the opening of a 19-kilometre road on Friday. Telefomin district administrator Joshua Himina said the road ended years of hardship and struggle people faced to access services and reach markets.
item.content:encoded:
FOR the first time, vehicles can now reach Tifalmin in Telefomin, West Sepik, following the opening of a 19-kilometre road on Friday.

Telefomin district administrator Joshua Himina said the road ended years of hardship and struggle people faced to access services and reach markets.

Himina said the district hoped that one day, Telefomin would be connected by road to the provincial headquarter in Vanimo.

“This dream must become part of every Telefomin’s people as you continue to live your life daily,” he said.

“In terms of modelling, district administration’s systems and process must function, public servants must do their job and the district must have infrastructures like road and others.”

The road was funded by the Ok Tedi Mining Limited (OTML) tax credit scheme with the support of the Telefomin district.

Himina told the people that they were trying their best to implement some projects in the 2023-2027 district plan.

“A vision of this plan is to ensure Telefomin is connected through telecommunication, infrastructure and roads that reach the border, reach the West Sepik provincial headquarter in Vanimo, connect other local level government in the district and onto Hela,” he said.

He also acknowledged people of Western for allowing Telefomin people to access services at at Tabubil and Kuinga.

Telefomin acting development authority chairman Stenson Mamsep acknowledged the late local MP Solan Mirisim and everyone who contributed to the road.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Prime Minister pays tribute to mothers
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has paid tribute to mothers across the country on Mother’s Day 2026, describing them as the “anchor of the family”. “Mother’s Day reminds us to reflect on the important role mothers play as the anchor of a family,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has paid tribute to mothers across the country on Mother’s Day 2026, describing them as the “anchor of the family”.

“Mother’s Day reminds us to reflect on the important role mothers play as the anchor of a family,” he said.

“If all mothers of our country are happy, then the country is a happy place.”

Marape urged men and boys to honour, protect, and care for women and girls and treat them with love and respect.

He reflected on the Biblical responsibility of men as the leaders within the family, and for them to rise to the challenge of building peaceful and loving homes.

Marape said Mother’s Day was not only a celebration of mothers, but also a moment for reflection on the role families played in shaping a safe, prosperous, and God-fearing nation.

He said Papua New Guinea’s identity as a Christian nation must be reflected in how families treated women and children.

“We claim that we are a Christian country, and the Christian worldview teaches us that love must be the foundation of the family,” he said.

Marape also expressed concern over the increasing cases of violence and abuse against women and girls in Papua New Guinea and called on communities to reject all forms of abuse and neglect.

“I see too many abusive husbands and too many abusive sons. This must stop,” he said.

“Men and boys of our country must rise to defend and protect our mothers and daughters.”

Marape also highlighted the Government’s continued commitment to supporting women and girls through policies and initiatives aimed at reducing gender-based violence, increasing women’s participation in education and economic opportunities, improving maternal health services, and strengthening law-and-order responses to domestic violence and abuse.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Parties told to uphold integrity
item.description: ACTING Electoral Commissioner Margaret Vagi has urged political parties to adhere to a code of conduct which supports the electoral process, fosters a peaceful campaign and promotes orderly elections. “The code of conduct is more than a set of principles; it  is a framework that requires candidates, as leaders, to take responsibility for the conduct of themselves and that of their scrutineers
item.content:encoded:
ACTING Electoral Commissioner Margaret Vagi has urged political parties to adhere to a code of conduct which supports the electoral process, fosters a peaceful campaign and promotes orderly elections.

“The code of conduct is more than a set of principles; it  is a framework that requires candidates, as leaders, to take responsibility for the conduct of themselves and that of their scrutineers and supporters,” she said.

“This framework is essential to help break the cycle of repeated misconduct by ensuring that political parties and candidates are aware of what is expected of them to uphold integrity and accountability.

“Leadership is not only about winning votes; it is about managing one’s camp with discipline, ensuring that supporters act lawfully, and scrutineers respect procedures.”

She said the commission, in partnership with the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates Commission and other stakeholders, would finalise a new code of conduct ahead of the General Election 2027 (GE27) for political parties, candidates, scrutineers and supporters to abide by.

Vagi said: “The code of conduct is not external to our work. It is integrated with the very framework of electoral administration.

“For the commission, it is a public commitment by candidates to support the integrity of the electoral process, foster a peaceful campaign environment and promote the orderly conduct of elections and become a tool of accountability.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: People must demand accountability from MPs: Marape
item.description: PEOPLE must demand accountability from their Members of Parliament (MPs) as districts and provinces have received “substantial funding” through their services improvement programme (SIP) funds, Prime Minister James Marape says. Speaking at Bialla in Nakanai, West New Britain on Friday, Marape warned that citizens would increasingly demand accountability from MPs ahead of General Election 2027.
item.content:encoded:
PEOPLE must demand accountability from their Members of Parliament (MPs) as districts and provinces have received “substantial funding” through their services improvement programme (SIP) funds, Prime Minister James Marape says.

Speaking at Bialla in Nakanai, West New Britain on Friday, Marape warned that citizens would increasingly demand accountability from MPs ahead of General Election 2027.

“By 2027, districts would have received between K70 million and K100 million,” he said.

“If the Government gives you K50 million, the people must see K50 million worth of work.”

In February, Finance Minister Thomas Opa revealed that the Government had paid K1.162 billion to provinces and districts in SIP funds under the control of MPs.

Opa said each of them had received an allocation of K10 million for their respective provinces (PSIP) and districts (DSIP).

He said the Finance Department payment records, as of December last year, confirmed that it paid out K960 million to the 96 districts and K220 million to the 22 provinces.

PSIP funds are paid to the provincial treasury and distributed at the discretion of the governor, while DSIP funds are paid to the district treasuries and distributed by the Open Member who chairs the district development authority.

Marape said that despite criticisms and economic challenges since 2019, including Covid-19 and global inflation pressures, his Government continued delivering development projects throughout the country.

“Since 2019, we have not stopped working,” he said.

“Even during difficult times, we continued investing in roads, schools, hospitals, and economic opportunities.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Liam interested. Panthers Yeo, Cleary, To’o, Kenny and Leota in the loop
item.description: PENRITH Panthers enforcer Liam Martin has officially opened the door to a possible move to the PNG Chiefs, declaring he will test his value on the open market once free agency begins on Nov 1. Australia media speculations surrounding the New South Wales forward’s possible move now intensifies, but Chiefs boss Lorna McPherson and general manager (football) Michael Chammas yesterday confirmed to The
item.content:encoded:
PENRITH Panthers enforcer Liam Martin has officially opened the door to a possible move to the PNG Chiefs, declaring he will test his value on the open market once free agency begins on Nov 1.

Australia media speculations surrounding the New South Wales forward’s possible move now intensifies, but Chiefs boss Lorna McPherson and general manager (football) Michael Chammas yesterday confirmed to The National that nothing is permanent from their end, referring to Martin’s interest.

It has now been said that the 19th NRL franchise could become a serious threat to Penrith ahead of their NRL debut in 2028.

Martin is among a long list of Panthers stars coming off contract at the end of the 2027 season, including captain Isaah Yeo, Nathan Cleary, winger Brian To’o, hooker Mitch Kenny and prop Moses Leota.

With the lucrative tax-free incentives, Australian reports suggest the club is targeting Penrith’s premiership-winning core as the foundation of its inaugural squad.

Speaking to Australian media, Martin admitted the idea of joining the Chiefs was becoming increasingly attractive.

“It’s not out of the question. It’s definitely of interest,” Martin said.

“Being part of something new and like, how much they love football, it’s incredible. And obviously Jarome going, it sorts of opens the doors for a lot of people.”

Martin also revealed discussions about the potential move have already taken place at home with his family. “I’ve discussed it with my partner and everything. Obviously would need a lot more details about it, but we certainly found it interesting,” he said.

Australian media outlets believe the PNG Chiefs’ financial advantages could dramatically reshape the NRL player market over the next two years, particularly with several elite Penrith Panthers players entering free agency at the same time.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Namo returns to boost Hunters campaign
item.description: THE PNG Hunters have confirmed the signing of experienced Papua New Guinean forward Sylvester Namo from the Brisbane Tigers for the remainder of the 2026 Hostplus Cup season. Namo joins the squad immediately, bringing leadership, professionalism, and valuable experience as the Hunters push forward in their campaign.
item.content:encoded:
THE PNG Hunters have confirmed the signing of experienced Papua New Guinean forward Sylvester Namo from the Brisbane Tigers for the remainder of the 2026 Hostplus Cup season.

Namo joins the squad immediately, bringing leadership, professionalism, and valuable experience as the Hunters push forward in their campaign.

A strong middle forward, Namo has played at elite levels both in Australia and overseas.

He previously featured in the North Queensland Cowboys system, gaining exposure in the Queensland Cup and NRL development programmes.

His career also includes a stint with the Castleford Tigers in England, further adding to his professional pedigree.

Hunters chief executive officer Scott Barker welcomed Namo’s return, highlighting the importance of his experience.

“Sylvester brings knowledge from both the Australian and United Kingdom rugby league systems. He will strengthen our forward pack on and off the field, while guiding younger players to meet professional standards,” Barker said.

He added that Namo’s return reinforces pathways for Papua New Guinean players to grow in elite environments.

Namo expressed gratitude for the opportunity to represent Papua New Guinea again. “I’m excited to join the Hunters and contribute for the rest of the season. My goal is to perform strongly and earn selection for the Kumuls at the upcoming Rugby League World Cup.

“The club has a clear vision toward 2028, and I look forward to being part of that journey,” he said.

The Hunters also acknowledged the Brisbane Tigers for their cooperation during the transfer process. With Namo’s addition, the Hunters aim to boost their forward strength and continue building a competitive squad for the future.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG golfers join professional ranks
item.description: A LANDMARK moment unfolded on Saturday at the Royal Port Moresby Golf Club as four Papua New Guinean golfers were officially accepted into the professional ranks of the PNG Golf Association (PNGGA). Morgan Anato, Soti Dinki, Gideon Takili, and Brian Takiri now join veteran professional Nelson Gabriel, forming a team poised to represent the nation on the international stage.
item.content:encoded:
A LANDMARK moment unfolded on Saturday at the Royal Port Moresby Golf Club as four Papua New Guinean golfers were officially accepted into the professional ranks of the PNG Golf Association (PNGGA).

Morgan Anato, Soti Dinki, Gideon Takili, and Brian Takiri now join veteran professional Nelson Gabriel, forming a team poised to represent the nation on the international stage.

Their elevation reflects years of dedication, discipline, and success in order of merit events both locally and abroad.

PNGGA president Ian Hodge described the announcement as a major sporting milestone, expressing hope that by 2028 these golfers will stand alongside the PNG Chiefs rugby league team, showcasing PNG’s talent across two global sports.

He urged corporate partners and Government to support the golfers’ journey, ensuring they reach their full potential.

The ceremony drew leaders from across the sporting and business community.

Nambawan Savings and Loan Society chairman John Cholai, CEO John Solok, NAC chairman Jacob Anga, and long time golf supporter Joe Kenken Mok congratulated the athletes, acknowledging the challenges of breaking into the professional circuit.

Cholai reminded them that professional golf is more than competition; it is a career requiring commitment.

Solok added that while PNGGA has guided the golfers this far, broader support is now essential.

Mok reflected on the 17-year journey that has finally turned a dream into reality for the Royal Port Moresby Golf Club and PNGGA.

Meanwhile, Kim Baldwin has been appointed PNGGA general manager, tasked with strengthening junior golf development.

Baldwin will oversee school clinics on a rotational basis in Port Moresby, Lae, and Mosa (West New Britain), ensuring children from all backgrounds have equal opportunities to learn the sport.

This announcement signals a new era for golf in Papua New Guinea, with five professionals ready to carry the nation’s flag into elite competition.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Indigenous team honoured at championships
item.description: THE Papua New Guinea Indigenous men’s team collected multiple match recognitions for their outstanding campaign at the 2026 National Indigenous Cricket Championships (NICC) in Mackay, Australia. Competing as an invitational side for the first time, the team impressed both on and off the field, earning the prestigious NICC Men’s Spirit of Cricket Award.
item.content:encoded:
THE Papua New Guinea Indigenous men’s team collected multiple match recognitions for their outstanding campaign at the 2026 National Indigenous Cricket Championships (NICC) in Mackay, Australia.

Competing as an invitational side for the first time, the team impressed both on and off the field, earning the prestigious NICC Men’s Spirit of Cricket Award.

Key honours included Tony Ura being named NICC Men’s Player of the Championship and earning selection in the Men’s Team of the Championship.

Michael Charlie was recognised as Westpac Player of the Round (Round 2), while Henao Thomas secured a place in the NICC Women’s Team of the Championship.

Round by round Player of the Match awards went to Tony Ura (Rounds 1 and 7), Michael Charlie (Round 2), Sese Bau (Round 4), and Hila Vare (Round 5).

PNG’s campaign featured back to back wins in rounds four and five, maintaining an unbeaten run mid tournament and showcasing the squad’s readiness for upcoming international commitments, including the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup Qualifier in Japan.

Meanwhile, four PNG Lewas players joined South Australia’s women’s team, further underlining the depth of PNG’s cricket talent and the strong culture of respect and sportsmanship that defines the national programme.

CPNG high performance manager and head coach Tim McCaskill said the Spirit of Cricket Award reflects “our standards”.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: High cost of chasing luck
item.description: LET us be honest: betting –whether sports apps, pokies, or daily ‘lucky number’ tickets – is designed for you to lose. Bookmakers do not get rich by paying out more than they take in.
item.content:encoded:
LET us be honest: betting –whether sports apps, pokies, or daily ‘lucky number’ tickets – is designed for you to lose.

Bookmakers do not get rich by paying out more than they take in.

True investment grows value: a small trade store, a garden, a child’s school fees, a term deposit.

Betting builds nothing – just K5, K20, K50 burned chasing a feeling.

K20 a day is K600 a month, K7,200 a year – lost.

Worse, near-wins trick your brain like a real win.

Problem gambling rewires you, making you chase losses and forget priorities.

Families break, paychecks vanish, and stress grows.

Your money exists to protect your family and future.

It is hard to earn and far too easy to lose.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Port Moresby must clean up its act
item.description: IT seems that many podcasters and influencers from Australia are flocking to Port Moresby following the Papua New Guinea Chiefs signing Jarome Luai, a National Rugby League star. We are seeing social media posts discussing Port Moresby and some of us are concerned.
item.content:encoded:
IT seems that many podcasters and influencers from Australia are flocking to Port Moresby following the Papua New Guinea Chiefs signing Jarome Luai, a National Rugby League star.

We are seeing social media posts discussing Port Moresby and some of us are concerned.

As it is, the National Capital District Commission (NCDC) and Members of Parliament ought to wake up and start a clean-up campaign and ban the chewing of betel nut in public spaces.

The filth and unhealthy piling up of rubbish everywhere in Port Moresby will be photographed by these visiting persons and the images of the dirty face of the capital city will be shown all over Australia and New Zealand.

That will be bad for the image of the capital city.

That means, it will be bad for the city and possible recruitment of the star players who may want to come here with their families.

Safety on the streets is something that NCDC and the city’s MPs also ought to fix immediately.

It would be terrible if a PNG Chiefs player or a family member is walking down the street at Boroko, Waigani, or Gordon, and gets robbed.

The city authority and MPs must wake up from their sleep and start cleaning up the street, and make the streets safer.

The public transport in Port Moresby too is nowhere near those of other cities in the Pacific.

The buses are old, people smoke in the bus and spit betel nut juice out of the door or window as if they are still living in an uncivilised society.

The city authority and MPs in Port Moresby ought to wake up and fix up the city before more media personnel from Australia or New Zealand come up to report on the infrastructure and environment.

Additionally, the drains in Boroko, Waigani and Gordon are filled with rubbish and are really smelly.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Real win in UPNG-ANU partnership renewal
item.description: THIS University of Papua New Guinea–Australian National University (UPNG-ANU) partnership renewal is genuinely good news for Papua New Guinea. Too often, we celebrate announcements that never deliver.
item.content:encoded:
THIS University of Papua New Guinea–Australian National University (UPNG-ANU) partnership renewal is genuinely good news for Papua New Guinea.

Too often, we celebrate announcements that never deliver.

The UPNG and the ANU renewed their long-standing partnership, reaffirming a shared commitment to strengthening economics and public policy education in PNG.

In just 10 years, UPNG’s School of Business and Public Policy has grown from fewer than 200 students to 1,200, and from five courses to 25.

This is not just a press release achievement – it means classrooms filled, lecturers trained, and graduates stepping into Treasury, Planning, Internal Revenue Commission, and provincial administrations with skills the country urgently needs.

The human stories highlight the real impact.

Take Joanne Cecil from Milne Bay – dux of her school, selected for ANU’s Crawford Summer School.

She returned inspired, seeing PNG as part of a larger global community.

Education should stretch our best young people and send them home to lead.

This partnership achieves that, one student at a time. It is also built on a model that works.

ANU scholars teach at UPNG alongside local staff, while students study in Australia and return home.

Knowledge transfer happens locally, building lasting capacity.

PNG’s biggest deficit is not money, it’s human capital.

Partnerships like UPNG–ANU close that gap, preparing the economists and policymakers who will shape the nation’s future, from managing liquefied natural gas revenue to addressing climate change.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: AI a tool, not replacement
item.description: ARTIFICIAL intelligence (AI) is increasingly being introduced into government systems worldwide, including in Papua New Guinea. It offers benefits such as faster decision-making, improved efficiency, and better planning in areas like disaster response, health, education, and national security.
item.content:encoded:
ARTIFICIAL intelligence (AI) is increasingly being introduced into government systems worldwide, including in Papua New Guinea.

It offers benefits such as faster decision-making, improved efficiency, and better planning in areas like disaster response, health, education, and national security.

However, these advantages come with serious risks if not properly managed.

AI can produce biased or incorrect outputs, weaken human judgement, and create accountability gaps, especially in sensitive defence and policy decisions.

As Papua New Guinea moves toward greater AI use in recruitment, procurement, and governance, strong safeguards are essential.

Human oversight, independent audits, fail-safe controls, staff training, and clear accountability systems must be in place to ensure AI supports, rather than replaces, responsible decision-making.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Neidab dedicates degree, award to late father
item.description: WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father. “I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Neidab Ulu went on stage to receive his degree and award, he was oblivious to the cheering, applause and claps as he was missing and thinking of the person he wanted to be there to witness his achievement – his late father.

“I lost my father during the first semester last year. He should have been attending and witnessing this ceremony.”

Neidab lost his father Ulu Rusini last year during the second semester of his final year – a critical time for any final year university student.

However, he managed to pull through and graduated with a Bachelor in Public Finance and Accountancy degree.

Neidab, 39, from Wanigela Village, Abau District, Central, is the second eldest of six children.

He was among those who graduated from the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy last Thursday.

To his employer, the Public Service Commission, he was an investment which paid off when he not only graduated but also named the top performing student in the Bachelor of Public Finance and Accountancy class.

He received the Academic Excellence Award after being sponsored by the PSC, where he has worked for 11 years, to pursue the degree while working as the acting Senior Budget Officer and Certifying Officer.

It was an opportunity he took seriously.

“I thank my employer, the Public Service Commission, for the investment and opportunity and trust placed in me to send me for further studies.”

His work involves budget support, expenditure management, and financial advisory duties. He knows that the degree will help him in his work and lift his performance.

“What motivated me to go back for further studies was to enhance my skills in my current duties on the processes in place to manage the public funds effectively and efficiently.”

Neidab attained a Certificate in Business Studies in 2007 and a Diploma in Business Studies in 2008 at the Port Moresby Business College.

In 2023, he graduated with a Diploma in Accounting from the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance (SILAG).

The loss of his father affected him. On top of that, he faced financial issues and tried his best to juggle his time between work, study, and family.

“I encountered financial challenges attending classes, attending to family matters, as well as work.”

At times when work and study demanded his attention most, he prioritised which one to tackle first.

“I’ve had clashes between my studies and my job. Some time, I had to attend to my work first and then my studies second because work is my bread and butter.”

The experience changed how he managed his time.

“It shaped me as a person to think critically and handle issues in a way that is balanced.”

At home, his wife Wendy Ulu and two sons Neidrolson who is in Grade Six, and Braden in Grade 4, endured nights when he had to burn the midnight oil.

Wife Wendy remembers those nights when they hardly went to sleep as Neidab would spend hours studying and keeping the lights on.

So she was happy and relieved to see him graduate with flying colours – a fitting reward for his hard work and perseverance. For the family, the hours of struggling to sleep are finally over.

Neidab dedicated the award to his employer, his late father, and his family.

“I dedicate the award to my employer and to my father who should have attended and witnessed this ceremony. And not forgetting my family who supported me throughout this journey.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s frontier judges: peace-building from the bench
item.description: IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges. Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.
item.content:encoded:
IN Papua New Guinea’s most conflict-torn provinces, an unlikely group of actors is stepping into the peacebuilding breach: resident National Court judges.

Armed with an unusually expansive constitutional mandate, some of these judges are deploying their powers not just to process the aftermath of violence, but to try to prevent it.

The scale of the challenge is hard to overstate. In Hela and Enga – two of the Highlands provinces hardest hit by inter-group conflict – sophisticated weaponry, warlord networks, and overwhelmed police have combined to produce a crisis that conventional state responses have struggled to contain.

Mobile squad deployments, special policing operations and draconian legislative announcements are all being experimented with.

But quietly, on the ground, judges are trying something different.

As reported in one of the daily newspapers on Feb 19, Justice Andrew Kostopolous, the resident National Court Judge in Hela, used a court-sanctioned inquiry into tribal fighting and lawlessness to confront the province’s deepening governance crisis head-on.

In proceedings marked by unusual candour, he issued bans on alcohol and the public carrying of bush knives, ordered the surrender of firearms, and named specific warlords as criminals subject to immediate arrest.

Justice Kostopolous also issued a human rights violation order against opposing factions in a tribal fight near Tari town, and delivered a stern warning that the province was “teetering on the edge of a constitutional crisis”.

“If the police aren’t doing their job, that’s a constitutional crisis,” he said.

“If the executive is not doing its job, that’s a constitutional crisis.”

These provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into “social engineers”.

The Supreme Court has affirmed that the Constitution is to be interpreted broadly, as “goal-oriented, purposive” and “liberal”, not in a “strict, technical and legalistic” manner.

Judges are urged to “think expansively and to be dynamic” and to “use judicial ingenuity to do justice”.

This is an unusual mandate, though not a unique one: judges in South Africa and India operate under similarly interventionist frameworks.

Three constitutional provisions in PNG are central to this logic.

Section 57 empowers the Supreme and National Courts to protect guaranteed rights and freedoms – either on application or on their own initiative.

Under this provision judges have summoned police to investigate alleged rights breaches, order the closure of prisons on humanitarian grounds, and directed law enforcement commissioners on the management of their facilities.

Critically, Section 57(5) extends this power to cases of “actual or imminent infringement”, allowing courts to make anticipatory orders before a rights breach has fully materialised.

Section 22 grants courts the power to enforce constitutional duties and to formulate procedures and mechanisms where existing frameworks are insufficient.

Section 225 obliges state agencies and service providers to actively support constitutional bodies, including the courts themselves.

Taken together, these provisions transform PNG judges from passive arbiters into what legal scholar Bal Kama describes as “social engineers” – actors empowered to identify systemic failures and act to address them.

In Enga, resident judge Justice Graham Ellis has been equally active.

 Last month, he issued a series of orders on his own initiative aimed at stopping tribal fighting in the Laiagam area.

These included a ban on carrying machetes and axes in public, the establishment of police roadblocks in the Mulitaka area, and orders for police to arrest anyone breaching National Court directives.

He explicitly framed these interventions as a matter of human rights enforcement under Section 57 of the Constitution, noting that the fighting affected rights to life, freedom, protection of the law, freedom of assembly, and freedom of movement.

Justice Ellis’s reasoning is worth quoting at length, because it illuminates the distinctive logic that underpins these interventions: “In many parts of the world, judges can do no more than consider the effects of criminal conduct, notably conducting trials after it is alleged that a crime has been committed. However, the inclusion of human rights provisions in the Constitution of PNG enables a judge to take a more active role by making orders, where it is considered appropriate, that can try to stop and even prevent criminal conduct.”

Both judges are thinking systemically, not just case by case.

Rather than waiting for individual matters to come before them and then ruling on them in isolation, they are using the bench as a vantage point from which to map the conditions generating violence – and to issue orders aimed at disrupting them.

This is a direct challenge to what one judge has described to us as the conventional posture of the justice system: “We tend to come in after everything has happened.

“We wait until it has happened and then clean up the mess.”

item.title: Liberty Liko becomes fourth doctor in family
item.description: HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be. “Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”
item.content:encoded:
HER parents are doctors. Her brother is one too. And so is her cousin. So when it came to choosing a career, Liberty Liko needed little persuasion on what she wanted to be.

“Medicine is all I have ever known. And since I was a little girl, I dreamt of becoming a doctor.”

Her father is Dr Osbourne Liko, a surgeon and urologist, who also served as Health Secretary and Medical Board chairman.

Her mother Dr. Mary-Julian Baki is a pediatrician who had served in Gulf, Chimbu and Port Moresby for a total of 23 years. She is now in private practice.

Her brother Dr Malachi Liko graduated in 2020 with a Bachelor in Medicine, Bachelor in Surgery (MBBS) degree. He currently works as a general practitioner.

Her first cousin on her father’s side, Dr. Joyce Sonoling, is a psychiatrist.

On top of that, her family’s close friends include Dr Mathias Sapuri and his daughter Dr Yvonne Sapuri.

Liberty, 23, was among 400-plus students who graduated from the University of Papua New Guinea School of Medicine and Health Science last week.

She had her face painted in Kairuku to honor her Mum, who is the first from Waima in Kairuku to become a doctor.

Liberty wore a Morobe bilum and necklace to honor her Morobe heritage and father who is from Pindu in Finschaffen.

She dressed in the same way as her mother when she graduated from UPNG in 1992. It was to honour her and other women choosing the profession.

“Now that my dream is a reality, I am overwhelmed with gratitude, first and foremost, to our ever merciful God, and to my backbone, my parents.

“I am equally thankful to my entire support network as this achievement is just as much theirs as it is mine. I’ve never had to look far for good role models and I aspire to be even half the doctor my parents are.”

Her parents supported her education into university. She also secured a Government scholarship.

She attended primary school in Australia because of her mother’s overseas work attachment. She later attended the Port Moresby Grammar School.

She completed grades seven to 12 at the St Joseph International College in Port Moresby in 2019 before she was accepted into UPNG on an academic excellence scholarship in 2020. But Covid-19 affected her studies. So to get into medical school, she dropped the Science degree and withdrew from UPNG.

She enrolled at the Pacific Adventist University in 2021 for the Science Foundation with the aim of getting into the School of Medicine and Health Sciences. She attended the UPNG medical school from 2022 to 2025.

She will decide what branch of medicine she is to pursue once she completes her residency and internship.

Dad Dr Liko is proud of her achievement.

“Liberty earned her achievements on her own merits unequivocally. In fact, she had an enormous shoe to fill throughout her journey in pursuit of her medical career. Nearly every lecturer knew her, a majority of doctors and nurses knew who she was.

“But she has always been herself – level-headed, cool, calm and collective. She respects everybody despite any opinion they may have on her. She is always considerate of her less fortunate school mates whose parents are not in Port Moresby.

“She earned her MBBS through disciplined study, always have resilient study habits, perseverance and consistent hard work over five demanding years.

“Despite her family’s medical backgrounds and public roles, I’m proud to say that she never sought advantage or special treatment, choosing instead to uphold fairness and independence.”

Mum Dr Baki described Liberty as confident, determined and an intelligent young lady who always strives for perfection.

“She was no dainty girl growing up and always fought with her six older siblings to be heard. I am very proud of her as a mother and a female professional.”

But she knows well what lies ahead of Liberty.

“This is just the beginning of a long journey of sleepless nights, no weekends or public holidays, missing out on family events and sacrifice of personal time. It is a calling to serve.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
